data.title: New York says Trump admin released Gateway tunnel funds after court order
data.description:
New York Attorney General Letitia James (D) said the Trump administration released nearly $130 million in funds for the Gateway tunnel project Wednesday following a judge’s order.

“This funding freeze was unlawful from the start. We took swift action in court, and now every dollar that was illegally withheld has been released,” James said in a statement. 

Last week, a judge’s order took effect requiring the administration to send New York and New Jersey the federal funds first cut off last fall. 

But the states continued to raise alarm as recently as Friday, saying they were still missing millions and the judge needed to act. 

She declined to do so, accepting the government’s contention that the funds were being processed and would be sent out of the Treasury Department’s payment system after the Presidents Day holiday. 

James’s statement confirms that has now taken place, staving off the need for the judge to intervene. 

In total, the states were waiting on about $200 million in frozen payments from the federal government when they sued. 

It is only the early stage of the legal battle, however; the 2nd U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals declined to immediately intervene, but it could do so in the coming weeks as the litigation progresses. 

And Trump continues to voice public opposition to the tunnel project, calling it a “future boondoggle” in a Truth Social post Monday. He also denied reports he demanded Penn Station be named after him to release the funding. 

“Please let this statement represent the fact that, under no circumstances, will the Federal Government be responsible for ANY COST OVERRUNS — NOT ONE DOLLAR!” Trump wrote. 

Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer (D-N.Y.), who met with the president last month about the frozen funds, also refuted claims he proposed adding Trump’s name to Penn Station and Washington Dulles International Airport.

data.title: FDA reverses course, will review Moderna's mRNA flu vaccine candidate
data.description:
The Food and Drug Administration (FDA) has backtracked its initial decision last week of refusing to review Moderna’s mRNA flu vaccine candidate after holding a high-priority meeting with the company.

Last week, the FDA said it refused to file Moderna’s application over regulatory issues. The company maintained that the refusal had nothing to do with the safety and efficacy of its vaccine candidate. On Wednesday, Moderna broke the news in a press release that the FDA had accepted its application for review following a high-priority, Type A meeting with the agency.

“To advance the review, Moderna proposed a regulatory pathway based on age, seeking full approval for adults 50 to 64 years of age and accelerated approval for adults 65 and older, along with a post-marketing requirement to conduct an additional study in older adults,” said the company.

Health and Human Services (HHS) spokesman Andrew Nixon said “the company refused to follow very clear FDA guidance from 2024 to test its product in a clinical trial against a CDC recommended flu vaccine to compare safety and efficacy.”

Nixon claimed Moderna exposed older trial participants to “substandard” care during the trial, though the company said the regulators had reviewed and cleared the design of the trial more than a year ago.

“As authorized under [Prescription Drug User Fee Act] and defined in FDA guidance documents, FDA held a Type A meeting with Moderna.  Discussions with the company led to a revised regulatory approach and an amended application, which FDA accepted. FDA will maintain its high standards during review and potential licensure stages as it does with all products,” Nixon on Wednesday said when reached by The Hill.

With this development, Moderna noted its flu mRNA candidate has now been accepted for review in the U.S., Canada, Australia and Europe.

Under HHS Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr.’s tenure, federal regulators have taken a somewhat hostile stance towards mRNA vaccines, cancelling millions in grant funding last year under the claim that these types of vaccines have been shown to be ineffective.

A federal vaccine advisory committee remade by Kennedy last year also voted to revise guidance for COVID-19 vaccines, the only mRNA shots on the market, to no longer give a blanket recommendation but limit immunization to individual decision-making.

Senior administration officials said last week that Kennedy had no part in Moderna’s request for review being refused, claiming he likely had no idea it was happening until news outlets reported on it.

data.title: Democrats less positive about party: Survey
data.description:
Democratic voters are expressing a less positive view of their party ahead of the midterm elections, according to recent polling data. 

In a new survey from the Associated Press-NORC Center for Public Affairs Research, about 7 in 10 Democratic voters said they have a positive view of the Democratic Party. 

These numbers are only slightly up from the 67 percent favorability rating Democrats gave their own party in a poll last October. The positive rating for the party hit its highest mark at 85 percent in several years in AP-NORC’s September 2024 poll. 

The party is relying on a groundswell of support from its base in the upcoming midterm elections. The first primaries will be held in early March in key states like North Carolina and Texas. 

In Texas, two Democratic candidates are competing in a closely contested race to oust Sen. John Cornyn (R). U.S. Rep. Jasmine Crockett (D-Texas) and state representative James Talarico have launched very different campaigns, with the congresswoman taking a more aggressive, anti-Trump stance than Talarico’s more moderate platform. 

Additionally, in Georgia, Democrats are trying to hold on to Sen. Jon Ossoff’s (D) seat. Three Republicans, including two U.S. congressmen and a former football coach, are facing off in the state’s Republican primary election on May 19. 

The state is also holding a crowded race to replace term-limited Republican Gov. Brian Kemp. Former Atlanta Mayor Keisha Lance Bottoms (D) is leading the polls in the Democratic race. The Republican primary ballot includes Georgia Secretary of State Brad Raffensperger, state Attorney General Chris Carr and President Trump-backed Burt Jones, the state’s lieutenant governor. 

The poll also indicated a low approval rating for Trump, with 61 percent of all respondents reporting a very or somewhat unfavorable view of the president. Only 34 percent of respondents indicated a very or somewhat favorable perception of Trump. 

The AP-NORC poll included responses from 1,156 adults between Feb. 5-8 with a margin of error of 3.9 percentage points. The margin of error for Democrats overall was 6 points.

data.title: Democratic-aligned Colorado group launches effort to redistrict after 2026
data.description:
A Democratic-aligned Colorado group is launching an effort to redistrict in the Centennial State after 2026 as the redistricting wars show no signs of letting up. 

Coloradans for a Level Playing Field filed four different versions for a ballot initiative on Tuesday, which would all largely ask voters to temporarily pass a new House map for the 2028 and 2030 elections, allowing them to go around the state’s independent redistricting commission until after the 2030 U.S. Census.  

Depending on which version is used, voters would need either at least 50 percent or 55 percent of the vote in order for it to pass. Democrats could net as many as three seats from a new map.  

“No one wanted to have to take this action — independent redistricting is the ideal,” Curtis Hubbard, a spokesperson for the group, said in a statement. 

“But with Donald Trump and MAGA Republicans actively working to rig Congressional elections, resulting in the potential gain of up to 27 seats in Congress, Colorado must join other states in countering this unprecedented power grab,” Hubbard continued. “We can sit back and do nothing, or we can take action to approve temporary maps that will help keep our elections on a level playing field.” 

The effort underscores how both parties are eyeing opportunities heading into the next few election cycles to create more pickup opportunities for themselves in the House. A handful of states have passed new maps or are using the referendum process to alter the congressional lines ahead of November. 

Virginia, Maryland and Florida are among the few remaining states that could pass new maps before November.  

The effort also spotlights the state’s rapid political shift as a once swing state to a more reliably blue tint statewide.

data.title: Live updates: DHS funding talks stall; Talarico in the spotlight over unaired Colbert interview
data.description:
The funding fight over the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) on Capitol Hill is at a standstill.

The White House on Tuesday indicated that negotiators are “still pretty far apart” after Democrats sent a Monday counterproposal outlining their demands for Immigration and Customs Enforcement reforms. The House and Senate are in recess.

With no end to the DHS shutdown in sight, House Democrats are gearing up to face off with President Trump ahead of his State of the Union address to Congress next week.

In Ohio, the House Oversight and Government Reform Committee is deposing former Victoria’s Secret CEO Les Wexner as part of its investigation into Jeffrey Epstein. Next week, the committee will hear from Bill and Hillary Clinton.

Texas Democratic Senate candidate James Talarico is making headlines over an unaired interview on CBS’s “The Late Show with Stephen Colbert,” which was later posted online. Colbert said CBS barred the interview from airing on TV, which the network has denied. 

Talarico’s Democratic opponent Rep. Jasmine Crockett (Texas) has characterized the saga as giving the state lawmaker  a “boost,” with early voting underway in the Lone Star State ahead of its March 3 primary.  

Trump on Wednesday afternoon will host a Black History Month reception at the White House while press secretary Karoline Leavitt will brief reporters at 1 p.m. EST.

Follow live below.

data.title: Unruly passenger forces Delta flight back to Houston
data.description:
HOUSTON (KIAH) – A Delta Air Lines flight leaving from William P. Hobby Airport early Wednesday morning was forced to turn around after what police initially described as a near cockpit breach.

According to the Houston Police Department, officers responded to reports of a disturbance onboard the aircraft and told Nexstar’s KIAH that there was an attempted breach at the cockpit. Authorities have not released additional details about what led to the incident or who was involved.

In a statement to KIAH, Delta said Flight 2557 from Houston (HOU) to Atlanta (ATL) returned to Hobby shortly after takeoff around 5:25 a.m. local time due to a passenger exhibiting “unruly and unlawful behavior” toward other customers.

“We can confirm that the customer approached crew and customers but did not make contact with or attempt to access the flight deck,” a Delta spokesperson said. The flight declared an emergency to receive priority handling and landed safely, where it was met by law enforcement.

Delta said the aircraft, a Boeing 717 carrying 85 customers and five crew members, later departed again and was expected to arrive in Atlanta approximately 90 minutes behind schedule.

“The safety of our customers and crew is paramount, and Delta has zero tolerance for unruly behavior. We apologize to our customers for this experience and delay in their travels,” the airline said.

Delta deferred further comment to law enforcement.

data.title: Trump family files to trademark use of name on airports
data.description:
The Trump Organization this week filed to trademark the use of President Trump’s name on airports, according to The Associated Press.

The move comes after Trump reportedly asked Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer (D-N.Y.) last month to support renaming Penn Station and Washington Dulles International Airport after him in exchange for his administration unfreezing $16 billion in federal funding for the Gateway tunnel project. 

The Trump Organization, which is run by the president’s sons, Donald Trump Jr. and Eric Trump, said in a filing with the U.S. Patent and Trademark Office that it does not seek any profit from the use of their last name.

“To be clear, the President and his family will not receive any royalty, licensing fee, or financial consideration whatsoever from the proposed airport renaming,” the company said in a statement, the AP reported.

Trump Organization also reportedly claimed the name is the “most infringed trademark in the world.” The trademark applications are for President Donald J. Trump International Airport, Donald J. Trump International Airport and DJT.

The Hill has reached out to the organization for further comment. 

Trump has pushed to rename multiple buildings and institutions after him since returning to the Oval Office, including the Kennedy Center and U.S. Institute for Peace. Congressional Democrats have slammed such attempts, including when Schumer reportedly denied the president’s request to rename Penn Station and Dulles.

Trump denied Monday that he “brought up” renaming Penn Station during his meeting with the New York Democrat, writing on Truth Social that it was proposed by “certain politicians and construction union heads.” Schumer has also refuted claims that it was his idea.

data.title: Rubio showed in Munich how respect, not confrontation, builds alliances
data.description:
At the Munich Security Conference last year, Vice President JD Vance delivered a blunt warning to Europe about its political and cultural trajectory. This weekend, Secretary of State Marco Rubio took the same stage and offered something very different: a call to restore shared identity and renew the transatlantic partnership.

Both leaders named the same reality — a West struggling with civilizational confidence, democratic stability, and cultural self-belief. But their contrasting approaches reveal something Washington too often forgets: tone is not merely cosmetic in diplomacy, but determinative in shaping outcomes. The difference between persuasion and alienation often boils down to respect.

In other words, diplomacy isn’t just about what we say. It is about whether anyone wants to listen when we say it.

Where Vance forced confrontation, Rubio invited cooperation. Europe’s response to each one illustrates why the distinction matters.

Rubio spoke to Europe as a civilizational partner, not a failed experiment. He reminded European leaders that the values America now seeks to defend — human dignity, ordered liberty and the rule of law — were first forged on European soil before crossing the Atlantic. The West’s inheritance is shared, he argued, and so is its responsibility.

His message was simple: You gave the world these principles — now stand with us in defending them.

Rubio did not minimize the West’s challenges. He spoke plainly about cultural drift, weakening security commitments and rising global threats. But he delivered those truths with respect and confidence in Europe’s capacity for renewal. Rather than condemn allies, he appealed to their highest ideals, demonstrating conviction without contempt.

European leaders heard affirmation rather than accusation and responded with standing ovation. Rubio preserved their dignity while advancing American interests — a reminder that respect is not merely a diplomatic courtesy, but a strategic advantage as well.

Respect lowers defenses. It invites cooperation. It allows hard truths to be heard rather than rejected.

Vance chose a different register in his Munich address last year. Where Rubio emphasized shared heritage, Vance had spotlighted Europe’s failures. He had challenged governing elites for disregarding voters, criticized migration policies imposed without public consent and warned of a leadership class increasingly detached from the civilization it inherited.

His argument was direct: Europe risks losing what it was given because it refuses to live by its own values.

The speech accomplished something important. Vance forced questions about national identity and cultural cohesion into the center stage of Western debate. He moved the Overton window on issues many leaders preferred to avoid.

But such confrontation comes with consequences. European officials responded with visible discomfort. Leaders already wary of American criticism became even more defensive. Allies who felt attacked became less cooperative, not more, proving that one can win an argument and still lose an ally.

The contrast between Rubio and Vance’s styles reflects two enduring instincts in leadership. One calls people upward through invitation. The other calls them out through correction. Both approaches can reveal truth, but each produces very different outcomes.

Rubio’s approach created space for change without humiliation. He reassured allies that America seeks partners, not subordinates. Vance’s approach, on the other hand, confronted realities that polite diplomacy often avoids and injected urgency into conversations that desperately need to be had.

The question is not which approach is more sincere. Rather, the question is which approach best advances American interests.

The West faces serious challenges: rising geopolitical competition, declining trust in institutions, cultural fragmentation and growing global instability. Meeting them requires both moral clarity and durable alliances.

Like a physician delivering a difficult diagnosis, leadership requires both honesty and bedside manner. The truth may be necessary, but how it is delivered determines whether the patient accepts treatment.

Europe’s reaction to Rubio’s speech suggests that respect remains one of America’s most powerful diplomatic tools. His tone did not dilute his message; rather, it strengthened it by grounding hard truths in our shared values, interests and history.

Rubio seems to understand what Vance tended to ignore: that allies persuaded by respect are stronger than allies coerced by pressure.

Confrontation alone rarely sustains alliances. Persistent rebuke turns friends into adversaries, even when the concerns being raised are legitimate.

Rubio demonstrated the strength of truth delivered with respect. Vance demonstrated the force of truth delivered through confrontation. Both approaches reveal real challenges, but only one strengthens the alliances necessary to meet them.

Politics, at its best, is about addition, not subtraction. It builds rather than fractures coalitions. It persuades instead of alienating. And in a world of rising global instability, America cannot afford to shrink its circle of friends.

Respect is not merely a diplomatic virtue — it is a strategic necessity. As Rubio showed in Munich, America leads best when it calls its allies to their highest ideals, strengthens shared purpose, and expands partnership. The future of Western leadership will depend not on how loudly we rebuke our allies, but on how effectively we inspire them to stand with us.

Denise Gitsham is a NewsNation contributor and former aide to George W. Bush. She is founder of Vitamin D Public Relations.

data.title: Pompeo: 'The Iranians have no interest in cutting a deal' with Trump
data.description:
Former Secretary of State Mike Pompeo on Tuesday said the Iranians are not interested in reaching a deal with the Trump administration as talks about dismantling their nuclear weapons program commence in Geneva, Switzerland. 

“The Iranians have no interest in cutting a deal. They are trying to drag this thing out. Any deal that they’d actually be prepared to sign isn’t going to be satisfactory,” Pompeo said during a radio appearance on the “Cats & Cosby Show” on WABC 770 AM.

“President Trump is not going to do what President Obama did: sign a weak agreement that allows the Iranians to continue to build their nuclear program out. That’s what [Iran has] offered so far with no ability to verify what they are actually doing,” he told hosts John Catsimatidis and Rita Cosby.

Former national security adviser John Bolton shared similar apprehensions during a Tuesday appearance on NewsNation’s “On Balance with Leland Vittert,” warning officials not to “dignify” or “legitimize” negotiations with Iran because “you can’t trust them.”

Bolton said Tehran’s leaders have never shown “any inclination” to give up their nuclear weapons program and said talks are worsening conditions in the Middle East. 

On the other hand, Pompeo, who served during the president’s first administration, said Trump’s talks with Iran are to make sure “we tried to see diplomacy all the way through.”

“In the end, I think this regime is recalcitrant. They will continue to try to be the world‘s largest state sponsor of terror. It’s going to require more pressure and more action from, not only us, but our friends in the Middle East as well,” the former secretary of state said on Tuesday. 

Pompeo added that Trump will likely deliver help to Iranian protesters, who he supported earlier in the year throughout weeks of demonstrations bucking the Iranian government. 

“President Trump made it pretty clear. He said … ‘Help is on the way.’ I’ve always known President Trump to deliver against commitments that he makes like that,” Pompeo said. 

“I am very hopeful that we will give the Iranian people the chance … to overthrow this regime. They want to live their lives, raise their families. They don’t want to live under a thugocracy,” he added.

data.title: Epstein files allegations may amount to 'crimes against humanity': UN experts
data.description:
A panel of United Nations experts suggested that allegations detailed in the millions of documents released by the Justice Department (DOJ) connected to its probe of convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein could amount to some of the most serious crimes under international law.

The group of experts, appointed by the U.N. Human Rights Council, said in a statement released Monday that the files signal “the existence of a global criminal enterprise” that engaged in the “systematic and large-scale sexual abuse, trafficking and exploitation” of women and girls.

“These crimes were committed against a backdrop of supremacist beliefs, racism, corruption, extreme misogyny, and the commodification and [dehumanization] of women and girls from different parts of the world,” they said.

“So grave is the scale, nature, systematic character, and transnational reach of these atrocities against women and girls, that a number of them may reasonably meet the legal threshold of crimes against humanity,” the panel added.

Crimes against humanity are defined under international law as when certain acts are committed “as part of widespread or systematic attack directed against any civilian population.” These acts — which include rape, sexual slavery, and murder, among other offenses — differ from war crimes in that they do not require armed conflict and can occur in peaceful times, according to the International Committee of the Red Cross.

The U.N. panel warned that some accusations contained in the documents released by the DOJ may meet that threshold.

“All the allegations contained in the ‘Epstein Files’ are egregious in nature and require independent, thorough, and impartial investigation, as well as inquiries to determine how such crimes could have taken place for so long,” they wrote.

The Trump administration has been criticized for its handling of the investigation into Epstein and his associate Ghislaine Maxwell, with critics arguing it has not been transparent in the batches of documents released.  

Lawmakers who reviewed the unredacted Epstein files at a DOJ office over the past week reported heavy, “unnecessary” redactions in what they saw. Trump officials have maintained the redactions are meant to ensure victims’ identities are kept private.

The U.N. panel echoed concerns about “botched redactions” that exposed victims’ information in previous disclosures — a blunder the Trump administration blamed on “technical or human error” when it announced it had taken down the documents and images.

“The grave errors in the release process underscore the urgent need for victim-centered standard operating procedures for disclosure and redaction, so that no victim suffers further harm,” they wrote.

Though not naming him directly, the experts also pushed back on President Trump’s suggestion that the country should move on from Epstein. They called on the administration to “end impunity for perpetrators” and pursue criminal probes where warranted.

“Failure by governments to effectively investigate, and prosecute those responsible for these crimes, including by complicity or acquiescence, where jurisdiction exists, risks undermining legal frameworks aimed at preventing and responding to violence against women and girls,” the group said.

“It is imperative that governments act decisively to hold perpetrators accountable,” the panel continued. “No one is too wealthy or too powerful to be above the law.”
